未来 · MIRAI · FUTURE

Your one-stop shop for future-ready learning.

Mirai Minds runs 60+ program formats across six verticals, all built on one belief: children learn best by making something real, and being proud to show it.

60+ program formats 6 verticals Grades K–12 Showcase-driven
The philosophy

What every program is built to deliver

Our curriculum SOP is the single reference for what each vertical delivers, not just its topic, but its experience, the wow moment it builds toward, and the outcome we want in the student. Speed is never the goal. A finished, polished result a child is proud to show a parent is the goal.

1

A wow moment, by design

Every multi-week program names its peak moment before week one is written (a showcase, a working demo, a real performance) and everything before it is paced as preparation for that moment.

2

Some projects move slowly, on purpose

Not every session ends in a finished object. Plans mark slow-build sessions (practice, iteration, no pressure to finish) against the one build-toward session where the wow lands.

3

Finished means finished

Whatever a student makes (a puppet, a pitch, a page) should look complete and cared for. Session time is deliberately reserved for finishing touches, not ended the moment a task is technically done.

Every one of our 60+ formats lives here

Creativity & Innovation

Collaboration & Team Work

The ability to need other people and make that work.

05

Leadership & Global Citizenship

A sense of responsibility to a world bigger than the self.

World Cultures ClubFestivals of the WorldYouth Leadership ForumCareer Compass
06

Physical & Mental Wellness

A calm, capable relationship with one's own body and focus.

Yoga & MindfulnessMixed Martial ArtsMove & GrooveDanceFun Fitness
The session architecture

Every session, every vertical, runs the same rhythm

A predictable open and close aren't just pedagogical bookends, they're behavioural ones. The shape below repeats in every Mirai Minds class.

01

Welcome Circle

A predictable opening ritual that signals the session has started, without raising a voice.

02

Hands-On Learning

The core skill, practised through making, never passive listening.

03

Group Projects

Named roles from the start, so collaboration is built in, not corrected later.

04

Reflection Circle

A calm, protected close where students name the choices they made.

Three sample programs, ready to run

The next three pages walk a full curriculum sample for each: the student journey, real session plans, and the actual worksheets and books our teams have built.

Entrepreneurship → Comic Design → AI Curriculum →
Critical Thinking & Problem Solving

Entrepreneurship: from a problem on paper to a Market Day stall

From Session 9, every team runs a real micro-venture with classmates as paying customers, using paper tokens as money, and the whole thing ends in a Market Day showcase. Every session ends with something tangible: a price, a prototype, a sale, a fix.

300+ hours of learningDifferentiated by grade groups
The learning journey

From a first problem to a Market Day stall

The program is a build: early sessions lay foundations, the middle deepens skill through real iteration, and the final sessions deliver the Market Day showcase the whole arc points toward.

1–8

Explore & Define

Explore entrepreneurship, identify a real problem, research a customer, and write a value proposition.

9–10

From Idea to Venture

Pitch problems, form teams, and work out costs and price through the ¥500 Challenge.

11–13

Build It

Design the smallest real version, build it from paper or as a service, and make a clear shopfront.

14–16

Sell & Improve

Test with real classmates using tokens, fix the biggest problem, and add simple branding.

17–22

Run the Business

Run trading rounds, sharpen a 30-second pitch and story, handle hard questions, and negotiate price.

23–25

Showcase

Plan and build a stall that shows the whole journey, then dress-rehearse it.

26–30

Sharper Skills

Strengthen teamwork, resilience, fairness, and growth thinking, through the team's own venture.

31–33

Market Day

Final setup, a soft launch with outside visitors, and the Market Day showcase and celebration.

  • Run the stall: pitch, trade, answer questions

The wow moment

Visitors walk each venture from problem to product to sales, the peak the whole arc was built toward.

The worksheets

Student-facing materials (tap to enlarge)

  • A real venture they designed, built, branded, and sold to actual customers
  • Experience pricing, testing, iterating, pitching, and negotiating
  • Confidence speaking and answering tough questions on their feet
  • A visible journey, from a problem on paper to a Market Day stall
Creativity & Innovation

Comic Book Design: from comic enthusiast to published creator

A sequential journey through storytelling, visual narrative, and professional publishing. Each creative build spans two full sessions, so students develop real depth, and it ends with a printed anthology and a student-led exhibition.

300+ hours of learningDifferentiated by grade groups
The progression arc

Six roles, one published creator

Comic Analyst→Visual Storyteller→Character Creator→Panel Architect→Illustrator→Publisher
1–6

Comic Language & Visual Literacy

Understand how comics communicate through panels, speech bubbles, and visual cues.

7–14

Character Design & Development

Create memorable, expressive characters with visual consistency and personality.

15–20

World-Building & Settings

Build immersive, visually consistent worlds using perspective, scale, and detail.

21–26

Story Structure & Narrative Planning

Develop compelling narratives structured for visual storytelling.

27–32

Visual Narrative Techniques

Master inking, colour, cover design, and publishing layout.

33

Publication & Celebration

The Comic Anthology Launch: students display finished comics and receive "Published Creator" certificates.

Sample session plans

Three creative builds

SESSIONS 1–2 · UNIT 1

Reading Comics Like a Detective

50 minutes
Objectives
  • See how comics tell stories through visual elements
  • Name key components: panels, bubbles, visual cues
  • Analyse how artists guide the reader's eye
Creative build

Comic Analysis Poster: annotate a published excerpt's techniques and explain how the artist tells the story without words.

SESSIONS 5–6 · UNIT 1

Speech Bubbles & Visual Effects

50 minutes
Objectives
  • Master dialogue, thought bubbles, and captions
  • Design sound-effect typography (BOOM! WHOOSH!)
  • Use text placement to guide reading order
Creative build

Silent Story with Sound: build a wordless 2-page comic, then add dialogue and effects with different bubble shapes.

SESSIONS 9–10 · UNIT 2

Facial Expressions & Emotions

50 minutes
Objectives
  • Draw one character across many emotional states
  • Understand eye / mouth / brow relationships
  • Keep a face consistent across drawings
Creative build

Emotion Expression Sheet: a character's face in 12+ emotions, kept as a personal character bible.

The worksheets

Student-facing materials (tap to enlarge)

  • A finished, printed comic in a class anthology
  • A complete character design bible they built themselves
  • A professional presentation at the launch exhibition
  • Reflection on their own creative growth
Critical Thinking · AI & ML

AI in Action: a published curriculum, grade by grade

Our AI curriculum spans Grades 1–12 as a spiral: the same big ideas revisited with rising depth. It's built as a series of full, illustrated student books. Featured here is the Grade 7 book, currently in production.

Grades 1–12 spiralFeatured: Grade 7Illustrated student book30-page sample
Grade 7 · AI in Action

A real book, not a slide deck

Every grade level is a designed, illustrated book that carries the student through AI concepts with characters, activities, and hands-on challenges, the same production quality a child would find on a shelf. The pages below are pulled straight from the Grade 7 sample.

Concept-ledActivity-richAge-appropriateFully illustrated
Inside the Grade 7 book

Sample pages (tap to enlarge)

Grades 1–12, one spiral

Grade 7 is one book in a full K–12 arc. Early grades meet AI through play and sorting; middle grades build and test simple models; senior grades investigate ethics, bias, and real systems. Each grade is its own published book.

Prepared for the Dreamtime Learning Online School listing
Teacher session plan

Pitch & Form Ventures

50 minutesModule · From Idea to Venture

Learning objectives

  • Communicate a problem and idea clearly in a short spoken pitch
  • Give and receive quick peer feedback on business ideas
  • Form small venture teams around a shared, real problem
  • Commit to a single venture concept the team will build and run

Materials

  • Students' notes from Sessions 1–8 (problem, customer, value proposition)
  • Smart screen for a 30-second timer and the Market Day goal
  • Scrap paper and pens. No printed materials required

Preparation

  • Display the end goal on screen: "Market Day, you sell to real classmates"
  • Set up a visible 30-second timer
  • Review students' problems from Session 5 to help match teams
  • Clear floor space so students can cluster into groups

Session flow

5 min
The venture begins

Their idea stops being a worksheet and becomes a real venture they will build, sell, and showcase on Market Day. Set the energy: today they pitch, then form teams.

15 min
Pitch round (whole class)

Each student gets 30 seconds: "I help ___ who struggle with ___." No slides, just speaking. Keep the timer strict to hold pace and energy.

20 min
Form ventures (teams)

Students cluster into teams of 2–3 around problems they share. Each team picks ONE problem and writes a single sentence: "Our venture helps ___ who struggle with ___ by ___."

10 min
Venture reveal

Each team reads its venture sentence aloud. The class responds with a thumbs-up and one quick question per team.

Differentiation

  • Confident students help a quieter peer shape and deliver their pitch
  • Students who struggle with English use a one-line written pitch or a drawing to point to
  • Students unsure of their idea join a team whose problem excites them
  • Larger classes run two half-class pitch circles at once to keep the pace

Notes for the teacher

  • Let students group by interest, not only friendship. If someone is left out, help match them to a problem they like.
  • Merging two similar problems into one venture is fine and often produces a stronger idea.
  • Keep the 30-second timer strict. The constraint keeps energy high and pitches sharp.
  • Save each team's venture sentence; it anchors every session that follows.
Teacher session plan

The ¥500 Challenge

50 minutesModule · From Idea to Venture

Learning objectives

  • Estimate the real costs of making a product or running a service
  • Set a price that covers costs and leaves a profit
  • Calculate and understand profit per sale (price − cost)
  • Make confident pricing decisions through a hands-on challenge, not a lecture

Materials

  • Scrap or recycled paper and pens
  • Smart screen to display the challenge and a timer
  • Optional: a small stack of paper tokens to demonstrate "buying"
  • No printed worksheets or templates needed

Preparation

  • Put the "You have ¥500 to start" challenge on the smart screen
  • Prepare one worked example (e.g. a paper bookmark: paper ¥10, sells for ¥50, ¥40 profit)
  • Re-read each team's venture statement from Session 9
  • Decide how teams record numbers: one shared sheet per team

Session flow

5 min
Opening challenge

"You have ¥500 to start. Spend it badly and you go bankrupt; spend it well and you make a profit." Do not define the terms yet; let the challenge create the need. Model once with the worked example.

15 min
Cost hunt (teams)

Teams list everything the offer needs and put a rough ¥ figure next to each, then total it. "Can you make your first version for under ¥500?" Circulate and probe: "Do you really need that? What could you reuse instead of buy?"

20 min
Set your price (teams)

Work out what one unit costs, then set a price a classmate would actually pay. Introduce profit per sale as price minus cost. Write cost, price, and profit-per-sale on the venture card.

10 min
Pricing showcase & close

Each team reads its price and profit-per-sale; the class votes "Would you pay that?" Use split votes to spark short discussion.

Differentiation

  • Advanced students find their break-even point and compare profit at two prices
  • Students who struggle with maths keep numbers small and round, with a partner or calculator
  • Students who struggle with English lean on the numbers and the on-screen example
  • Quieter students each own and report one cost

Notes for the teacher

  • Resist explaining the terms up front. Let the ¥500 challenge create the need, then name the concepts as teams hit them.
  • Do not correct prices; ask "would a classmate actually pay that?" A price the class rejects teaches more than one you fix.
  • Keep the maths small and real. The goal is confident decision-making, not perfect accounting.
  • Save each team's venture card; they build directly on it next session.
Teacher session plan

Market Day & Celebration

50 minutesModule · Market Day

Learning objectives

  • Showcase the complete venture journey to visitors
  • Run the stall: pitch, trade, and answer questions
  • Celebrate the whole class's achievement
  • Reflect with pride on growth and learning

Materials

  • Completed stalls and the journey wall
  • Paper tokens and a space set up for visitors
  • Optional: simple recognition (certificates or a class cheer)

Preparation

  • Set up the market space and journey wall prominently
  • Confirm visitors (another class, other teachers, or families)
  • Distribute tokens
  • Plan recognition for every team

Session flow

5 min
Welcome to Market Day

Explain how the showcase works: visitors walk each venture's journey, ask questions, and can "buy."

30 min
Market Day (whole class)

Full showcase. Visitors explore each venture from problem to product to sales; teams pitch, trade, and answer questions.

15 min
Celebrate

Gather at the journey wall, recognise every team, and have each student say one thing they are proud of.

Differentiation

  • Advanced students mentor a nervous teammate through the rush
  • Students who struggle with English let their visual stall and rehearsed lines carry the day
  • Nervous students draw on the rehearsal and soft launch that prepared them

Notes for the teacher

  • This is a learning-journey showcase, not a stage performance. Spotlight effort, growth, and what each team learned, not just the best seller.
  • Make sure every student is recognised by name and gets their proud moment.
  • Photograph stalls and the journey wall to close the program well.
Teacher session plan

Reading Comics Like a Detective

50 minutesUnit 1 · Sessions 1–2Part 1 of 2

Students become "visual detectives," learning to read comics not just for story but for how the artist communicates through line, colour, composition, and panel arrangement.

Learning objectives

  • Understand how comics use visual elements to tell stories
  • Identify and name key components (panels, speech bubbles, visual cues)
  • Analyse how artists guide the reader's eye through a page
  • Begin selecting and annotating a comic excerpt for analysis

Materials

  • Printed comic excerpts in 3–5 styles (superhero, slice-of-life, fantasy, manga-influenced)
  • Magnifying glasses or enlarged printed panels
  • Comic vocabulary cards, coloured pencils or markers
  • Sketchbooks or analysis worksheets, chart paper

Session flow

5 min
Opening hook

Show a panel with the dialogue removed: "What is happening here? How do you know?" Introduce the class as comic detectives.

10 min
Mini-lesson: vocabulary

Define panel, gutter, speech bubble, thought bubble, caption, sound effect, line weight, perspective, and composition. Students point out examples; highlight them on chart paper.

20 min
Guided practice: detective analysis

Observe a 3–4 panel excerpt silently, then annotate it together (reading path, techniques, emotional impact) and discuss "How does the artist make us feel?"

10 min
Independent exploration

Each student selects one excerpt and makes first observations in their sketchbook.

5 min
Closing

A few students share which comic they chose and why; preview the analysis poster they will build next.

Differentiation

  • Advanced learners take more complex excerpts and find multiple techniques in one panel
  • Emerging learners use simpler styles and picture-based vocabulary cards, working with a partner
  • Visual learners use colour-coded annotations and vocabulary posters
  • Kinesthetic learners trace reading paths on large printed panels

Facilitator tips

  • Magnifying glasses make observation feel like real detective work, which is engaging and motivating.
  • Celebrate small observations: "Great eye, you noticed the line weight changed there."
  • Keep the tone playful and exploratory, not academic.
Teacher session plan

Speech Bubbles & Visual Effects

50 minutesUnit 1 · Sessions 5–6Part 1 of 2

Students learn that different bubbles and text carry different meaning (speech, thought, caption, and stylised sound effects) and plan their "Silent Story with Sound" project.

Learning objectives

  • Understand different types of speech bubbles and their meanings
  • Learn how text placement guides reading order
  • Explore sound-effect typography and visual representation
  • Begin planning a wordless-to-worded comic project

Materials

  • Comic excerpts showing speech, thought, caption, and sound-effect text
  • A bubble-type reference chart and examples of creative sound-effect lettering
  • Blank 2-page comic templates (4–6 panels per page)
  • Pencils, erasers, coloured pencils, sketchbooks, chart paper

Session flow

5 min
Opening hook

Show a panel with text removed, then with text added: "How does the text change what you understand?"

12 min
Mini-lesson: text & bubbles

Introduce speech, thought, caption, whisper, and scream bubbles; sound-effect lettering (BOOM, SPLASH, WHOOSH); and how placement guides reading order.

18 min
Guided practice: bubble & text analysis

Identify and label bubble types and reading order on an excerpt, then practise drawing stylised sound effects.

10 min
Project launch: Silent Story with Sound

Brainstorm a 2-page story (8–12 panels) and plan its dialogue, thoughts, and sound effects.

5 min
Closing

Reflect on how bubble shape, lettering, and placement all carry meaning; preview drawing the wordless comic.

Differentiation

  • Advanced learners use five or more bubble types and several lettering styles
  • Emerging learners focus on speech bubbles and one sound effect, with sentence starters
  • Visual learners use colour-coded bubble types
  • Kinesthetic learners shape bubbles with their hands and act out dialogue

Facilitator tips

  • Demonstrate sound effects with big gestures and voices: "BOOM is loud and bold; SPLASH is flowing and wavy."
  • Use published comics to show how professionals place bubbles and text.
  • Remind students that pictures tell the story and text just helps, so keep it simple.
Teacher session plan

Facial Expressions & Emotions

50 minutesUnit 2 · Sessions 9–10Part 1 of 2

Students discover that eyebrows, eyes, and mouth work together to show emotion, and plan an Emotion Expression Sheet drawing one character across twelve or more feelings.

Learning objectives

  • Understand how facial features communicate emotion
  • Learn the relationships between eyes, mouth, and eyebrows
  • Analyse how different artists draw the same emotion
  • Begin planning an emotion expression sheet

Materials

  • Comic excerpts showing different emotions
  • A facial-expression reference chart
  • Pencils, erasers, sketchbooks, chart paper
  • Mirrors for students to observe their own expressions

Session flow

5 min
Opening hook

Make exaggerated expressions silently: "What am I feeling? How can you tell?"

12 min
Mini-lesson: features & emotion

How eyebrows, eyes, and mouth each shift, and how they combine. Analyse published panels feature by feature.

18 min
Guided practice: mirror work + drawing

Students study their own faces in mirrors, then draw one face across six basic emotions, modelled step by step.

10 min
Project launch: Emotion Expression Sheet

Choose a character and begin practising its face across twelve or more emotions.

5 min
Closing

Reflect on how faces communicate; preview the full expression sheet they will complete next.

Differentiation

  • Advanced learners draw fifteen or more emotions and create subtle variations (happy vs very happy)
  • Emerging learners focus on six basic emotions with face templates and a partner
  • Visual learners use colour-coded facial features and reference charts
  • Kinesthetic learners use mirrors and gestures to feel each expression

Facilitator tips

  • Exaggerate when demonstrating: "Happy is a big smile; sad is a droopy mouth."
  • Celebrate observations: "Great eye, the eyebrows angle inward for anger."
  • If a student says "I cannot draw faces," start from a circle, two dots, and a line, then change the mouth.
